name: Mach Number (Ma)
desc: Dimensionless ratio of local flow speed (V) to the speed of sound (c) in that fluid.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Don't assume gases are ALWAYS compressible! If Ma < 0.3 (like air under 100 m/s), density changes are < 5% and the gas MUST be treated as an INCOMPRESSIBLE fluid.

name: Specific Weight (γ)
desc: The weight of a unit volume of a substance.
validations: 📌 Standard gravity 'g' is approx 9.81 m/s² at sea level.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Density (ρ) is constant, but Specific Weight (γ) changes with location because 'g' changes! Never confuse it with Specific Gravity.

name: No-Slip Condition
desc: A fluid in direct contact with a solid surface 'sticks' to it due to viscous effects, causing the boundary layer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The absolute velocity of the fluid isn't always zero! The RELATIVE velocity is zero. If a plate is moving at 5 m/s, the fluid particle exactly at the wall is ALSO moving at 5 m/s.

name: Dimensional Homogeneity
desc: All additive terms in an engineering equation must have the exact same primary dimensions.
validations: 📌 Used strictly to catch errors in formula derivation.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: You CANNOT add standard Energy (kJ) with Specific Energy (kJ/kg) directly! Always multiply the specific term by mass (m) first before adding. (Classic silly mistake in Thermodynamics/Fluid equations).

name: Newton's Unity Conversion (Force)
desc: The fundamental conversion ratios for force in SI and English systems.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In the English system, never cancel lbf with lbm directly! You MUST use the conversion factor gc = 32.174 (lbm·ft)/(lbf·s²) to balance the units.

name: Ideal Gas Equation of State
desc: Relates absolute pressure, density, and absolute temperature for a gas.
validations: 📌 R is the specific gas constant (Universal Gas Constant / Molar Mass).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Temperature MUST be in Kelvin (or Rankine) and Pressure MUST be Absolute! Never use Gauge pressure or Celsius here.

name: Coefficient of Compressibility (Bulk Modulus, K)
desc: Measures a fluid's resistance to compression. Higher K means more incompressible.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For an IDEAL GAS, the isothermal bulk modulus K is exactly equal to its absolute pressure (K = P). Don't waste time deriving it in the exam!

name: Coefficient of Volume Expansion (β)
desc: Measures how much density changes with temperature at constant pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For an IDEAL GAS, β = 1/T. Students often put T in Celsius here. It MUST be in Kelvin! If T = 27°C, β = 1/300 K⁻¹, NOT 1/27.

name: Newton's Law of Viscosity
desc: Relates shear stress to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) for Newtonian fluids.
validations: 📌 μ is Dynamic Viscosity (Pa·s). 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Temperature effect is a classic MSQ! Liquid viscosity DECREASES with temp (cohesive forces weaken). Gas viscosity INCREASES with temp (molecular collisions increase).

name: Kinematic Viscosity (ν)
desc: Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density. Represents momentum diffusivity.
validations: 📌 Unit is m²/s. 1 Stoke (St) = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For gases, dynamic viscosity (μ) is mostly independent of pressure, but kinematic viscosity (ν) DEPENDS strongly on pressure because density (ρ) changes!

name: Pressure Jump Across Curved Surface
desc: The pressure difference between the inside and outside of a curved liquid interface.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A soap 'bubble' has TWO surfaces (inner and outer), hence 4σ/R. A liquid 'droplet' only has ONE surface, hence 2σ/R. Also, the formula uses Radius (R), examiners often give Diameter (D) to trick you.

name: Capillary Rise/Fall (h)
desc: The elevation or depression of a liquid in a narrow tube due to surface tension.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If the contact angle ϕ > 90° (like Mercury in glass), cos(ϕ) is negative, resulting in a Capillary FALL (depression). Always double-check if the question gave 'Diameter' instead of 'Radius' (R)!

name: Cavitation Condition
desc: Formation of destructive vapor bubbles when local static pressure in a flowing liquid drops below its vapor pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Cavitation is driven by PRESSURE drops (e.g., in pump impellers or venturi throats), not just temperature increases. It causes severe structural pitting when the bubbles collapse.
